Name: info_theoretic_neural_pruning
Title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Neural Network Pruning
Short Hypothesis: Neural network pruning can be formulated as a rate-distortion problem, where the goal is to minimize the number of parameters while preserving the mutual information between the input and output. We hypothesize that information-theoretic bounds can be used to guide the pruning process and achieve optimal trade-offs between model size and accuracy.
Related Work: Recent works such as Spectral Quantization Routing and Geometric Signal Separation have explored training-free methods for precision allocation and signal separation. However, these approaches do not provide a clear understanding of the fundamental limits of neural network compression. Our proposal builds upon the concept of rate-distortion theory and information-theoretic bounds to provide a principled approach to neural network pruning.
Abstract: We propose an information-theoretic framework for neural network pruning, where the goal is to minimize the number of parameters while preserving the mutual information between the input and output. We derive bounds on the achievable rate-distortion trade-off and propose a pruning algorithm that achieves these bounds. Our approach provides a principled way to balance model size and accuracy, and has the potential to lead to significant reductions in computational resources required for neural network deployment.
Experiments: Derive information-theoretic bounds on the achievable rate-distortion trade-off for neural network pruning
Propose a pruning algorithm that achieves these bounds
Evaluate the performance of the pruning algorithm on benchmark datasets and compare to state-of-the-art methods
Investigate the effect of different distortion measures and pruning strategies on the trade-off between model size and accuracy
Risk Factors and Limitations: The complexity of the derivation of information-theoretic bounds
The sensitivity of the pruning algorithm to hyperparameters and initialization
The potential for over-pruning or under-pruning, leading to suboptimal trade-offs between model size and accuracy
